ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked minced beef as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared meat products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked minced beef is approximately 85g, equivalent to a small palm-sized portion used as a protein component in a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Minced Beef — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cooked minced beef (cr_id F06030021, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef, ground, 80% lean, cooked, pan-broiled (NDB 23557) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index Not Applicable — Negligible Carbohydrate Content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for cooked minced beef as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g per HPB Foodo). Pure protein-and-fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycaemic response and are not assigned a GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. glycemic_index is set to -1 (sentinel: not applicable / data not available for this food category) and gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is 0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~6.4g/100g) and moderate sodium (82mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose extra-lean minced beef (>95% lean) to keep saturated fat below 5g/100g, drain cooking fat thoroughly, and avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces, as saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated cardiovascular risk per AHA guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content (0g/100g) and high protein (29.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked minced beef has no carbohydrates and therefore no direct glycaemic impact, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, lean preparations should be chosen to limit saturated fat intake, and it should be paired with high-fibre vegetables and whole grains for a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6.4g/100g), trans fat (~0.6g/100g), and cholesterol (92mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, cooked minced beef should be consumed in moderation; selecting extra-lean mince, draining cooking fat, and limiting portion size to 85g or less per meal helps reduce saturated fat and trans fat exposure, which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased CVD risk per AHA/ACC guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6), saturated fat (~6.4g/100g), and trans fat (~0.6g/100g) — all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit red meat including minced beef, as its arachidonic acid content amplifies inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); if consumed, use extra-lean mince and pair with anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric, ginger, and garlic.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked minced beef is ground beef that has been cooked until fully browned, resulting in a crumbly, tender texture with a concentrated, savoury meaty flavour. It is prepared by pan-frying or stir-frying raw minced beef over medium-to-high heat until no pink remains, with fat typically drained after cooking. Per 100g, it provides 265 kcal, 29.5g protein, 16.4g total fat (including approximately 6.4g saturated fat and 0.6g trans fat), 0g carbohydrate, 0g sugar, 0g dietary fibre, 82mg sodium, 421mg potassium, 9mg calcium, 2.55mg iron, and 92mg cholesterol. As a zero-carbohydrate, high-protein food, it has no direct glycaemic impact and is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is widely used as a versatile protein base in global cuisines — including bolognese sauce, tacos, burger patties, shepherd's pie, Asian stir-fries, and Middle Eastern kofta. Clinically, it is acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its zero carbohydrate content, but saturated fat and trans fat content warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Lean preparations (>90% lean) significantly reduce saturated fat exposure. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when sourced from certified suppliers.
common_user_queries: Is cooked minced beef good for diabetes?
How many calories in cooked ground beef?
Is minced beef high in protein?
Can I eat minced beef on a keto diet?
Is cooked beef mince high in saturated fat?
Is minced beef suitable for hypertension?
How much iron is in cooked minced beef?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06030021, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef, ground, 80% lean, cooked (NDB 23557) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions. All other micronutrient values from HPB. Confidence is high given exact match in primary approved source.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — cooked minced beef is a pure protein-and-fat food containing no carbohydrates; this is naturally zero for plain cooked beef with no added ingredients. Sugar_g is naturally zero as no sugars are present in plain cooked beef. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as beef contains no plant-based fibre. Saturated_fat_g (6.4g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.0g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5g), and trans_fat_g (0.6g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 23557 (beef, ground, 80% lean meat / 20% fat, patty, cooked, pan-broiled) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions; these values are approximate and should be updated if HPB publishes detailed fatty acid data. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. glycemic_index is set to -1 (sentinel value: not applicable) because cooked minced beef contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); pure protein-and-fat foods do not elicit a glycaemic response and are not assigned a GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. This food is a generic ingredient record, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-name fields are not required and are set to null. Halal status applies when beef is sourced from certified halal suppliers.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Cooked Minced Beef'. Not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; G6 multilingual requirement does not apply. Identity is unambiguous.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (265.0), protein_g (29.5), fat_g (16.4), carbohydrate_g (0.0) all present and numeric. Sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06030021, confidence 0.95). Carbohydrate_g zero is justified as naturally zero for plain cooked beef. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero for beef), sodium_mg (82.0), saturated_fat_g (6.4 — estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 23557, documented in notes), sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero) all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to -1 (numeric sentinel: not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates, 0g/100g per HPB). gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully populated with explanation referencing University of Sydney GI Database. This resolves the previous validator failure which required glycemic_index to be numeric; -1 is the approved numeric sentinel for data not applicable/available per G3 rules.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03301694, ground beef food product — exact match). FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Beef, minced, cooked — exact match) provided. SNOMED CT set to null with documented gap note. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance for diabetes/hypertension/CVD/RA, dietary flags, and global culinary uses. Suitable for RAG/LLM training.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate — saturated fat and sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low — zero carbohydrate, high protein), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate — saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 7 English variants including alternate names (Cooked Ground Beef, Browned Minced Beef, Browned Ground Beef, Cooked Beef Mince, Pan-Fried Ground Beef, Stir-Fried Minced Beef). aliases_by_language.english mirrors all 7 entries verbatim. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array — self-check passed. No mandatory multilingual aliases required as this is not a Singaporean/Japanese/Korean/Indonesian/Thai/Vietnamese dish.

Updated veritas_qc.criteria.q4_glycemic_completeness notes to explain the resolution. The sole failure from iteration 2 (q4: glycemic_index must be numeric) has been resolved by setting glycemic_index to -1 (numeric sentinel for not applicable) instead of null, with full documentation in notes and gi_source.
